prompt: In New York, real estate salesperson licenses are issued and overseen primarily by:

options.text: The Department of State (DOS) Division of Licensing Services
The Internal Revenue Service exclusively
Local zoning boards only
The Federal Reserve Board

explanation: NY DOS Licensing Services regulates real estate salesperson and broker licenses under Article 12-A.

distractorExplanations.b: IRS handles federal taxes, not NY real estate licenses.
distractorExplanations.c: Zoning boards regulate land use, not salesperson licensing.
distractorExplanations.d: The Federal Reserve does not license NY salespersons.

prompt: A New York real estate salesperson may lawfully receive compensation only from:

options.text: The buyer directly as an unlicensed side fee
The broker with whom the salesperson is associated
Any title company as a secret bonus
A FSBO seller without broker knowledge when prohibited

explanation: Salespersons are paid through their sponsoring broker; bypassing the broker violates license law.

distractorExplanations.a: Direct unlicensed fees are improper.
distractorExplanations.c: Secret title bonuses can violate RESPA and ethics rules.
distractorExplanations.d: Broker supervision and compensation rules still apply.

prompt: Before engaging in activities requiring a license, a New York salesperson must:

options.text: Only post ads on social media without affiliation
Practice independently without any broker
Be associated with a licensed broker and hold an active license
Use a friend's broker pocket card without association

explanation: A salesperson cannot practice independently; association with a licensed broker is required.

distractorExplanations.a: Advertising licensed services still requires proper affiliation.
distractorExplanations.b: Independent practice requires a broker license.
distractorExplanations.d: Using another person's credentials is illegal.

prompt: Commingling occurs when a broker:

options.text: Deposits earnest money into a proper escrow account
Keeps accurate escrow ledgers
Discloses agency relationships in writing
Mixes client trust/escrow funds with personal or operating funds improperly

explanation: Mixing trust money with personal or operating funds is a serious violation; conversion is misuse of those funds.

distractorExplanations.a: Proper escrow deposit is required compliance.
distractorExplanations.b: Good records support compliance.
distractorExplanations.c: Agency disclosure is a separate duty.

prompt: Which statement about New York pocket cards is generally correct?

options.text: Licensees should carry their pocket card when engaging in licensed activity and produce it upon request
Pocket cards are optional wallpaper decorations only
Only attorneys need pocket cards statewide
Pocket cards replace the need for any broker supervision

explanation: DOS-issued pocket cards identify licensed status; licensees must be able to show them when practicing.

distractorExplanations.b: Pocket cards are official credentials, not decorations.
distractorExplanations.c: Salespersons and brokers need them, not only attorneys.
distractorExplanations.d: Supervision requirements remain regardless of the card.

prompt: If a sponsoring broker's license is suspended or revoked, associated salespersons generally:

options.text: Automatically become brokers overnight
Cannot continue licensed activity under that broker until properly re-associated
May keep practicing under the suspended broker
Lose their Social Security numbers

explanation: Salesperson authority depends on association with an active broker; suspension/revocation stops that pathway.

distractorExplanations.a: Broker status is not automatic.
distractorExplanations.c: Practicing under a suspended broker is not permitted.
distractorExplanations.d: SSNs are unrelated to license status.

prompt: Unlicensed assistants in New York generally may:

options.text: Negotiate purchase offers independently with buyers
Show properties and discuss terms as if licensed
Perform clerical tasks that do not require a license (e.g., scheduling, filing)
Receive commission splits for brokerage services

explanation: Unlicensed staff may do support work but may not perform activities that require a real estate license.

distractorExplanations.a: Negotiating offers is licensed activity.
distractorExplanations.b: Showing/discussing terms typically requires a license.
distractorExplanations.d: Commission splits for brokerage services require licensure.

prompt: A New York salesperson's license renewal typically requires:

options.text: Never completing any continuing education
Paying only a parking ticket
Automatic lifetime renewal with no filing
Meeting DOS renewal and continuing education requirements on the prescribed cycle

explanation: Active status depends on timely renewal and required CE under DOS rules.

distractorExplanations.a: CE is generally required for renewal.
distractorExplanations.b: Parking tickets are unrelated.
distractorExplanations.c: Licenses are not lifetime without renewal.

prompt: Blind ads that hide the broker's identity are generally:

options.text: Prohibited; ads must properly identify the brokerage
Required for all listings
Allowed only if the salesperson's home address is used alone
Encouraged to increase mystery marketing

explanation: Advertising must not mislead and must properly identify the licensed brokerage.

distractorExplanations.b: Transparency, not anonymity, is required.
distractorExplanations.c: Home-address-only ads that conceal the broker are improper.
distractorExplanations.d: Mystery ads that hide the firm violate advertising rules.

prompt: Kickbacks for settlement services are restricted under federal RESPA; a lawful referral fee generally requires:

options.text: Secret payments to unlicensed people for brokerage services
Compliance with RESPA and New York rules, including disclosure and licensing constraints
Sharing commissions with unlicensed finders for licensed services always
No documentation ever

explanation: Fee-sharing for real estate and settlement services is tightly regulated; secret unlicensed payments are illegal.

distractorExplanations.a: Secret unlicensed payments violate law.
distractorExplanations.c: Unlicensed commission sharing for brokerage services is prohibited.
distractorExplanations.d: Documentation and disclosure matter.

prompt: Article 12-A of the New York Real Property Law primarily addresses:

options.text: Federal bankruptcy exemptions only
Coastal fishing quotas
Licensing and regulation of real estate brokers and salespersons
Interstate trucking hours of service

explanation: Article 12-A is the core statutory framework for NY real estate brokerage licensing.

distractorExplanations.a: Bankruptcy is a separate body of law.
distractorExplanations.b: Fishing quotas are unrelated.
distractorExplanations.d: HOS rules are FMCSA, not Article 12-A.

prompt: A net listing arrangement in New York is generally:

options.text: The only lawful listing form
Required by DOS for every sale
Mandatory for condominium resales
Illegal / prohibited as a listing method

explanation: Net listings (seller gets a set net; broker keeps the excess as commission) are prohibited in New York.

distractorExplanations.a: Other lawful listing forms exist (exclusive, open, etc.).
distractorExplanations.b: DOS does not require net listings.
distractorExplanations.c: Condo resales are not exempted into net listings.

prompt: When must a New York licensee generally provide agency disclosure?

options.text: At the first substantive contact with a buyer or seller regarding a specific property, as required
Only after the deed is recorded
Never, if the deal is under $1 million
Only when the salesperson feels like it

explanation: NY agency disclosure timing rules require early, proper disclosure of the licensee's role.

distractorExplanations.b: Disclosure is required before closing/recording stages.
distractorExplanations.c: Price does not waive disclosure.
distractorExplanations.d: Disclosure is a legal duty, not optional mood.

prompt: Maintaining a place of business for a New York broker generally means:

options.text: Using only an anonymous P.O. box with no required presence when rules require an office
Maintaining a compliant office that meets DOS/business requirements for the brokerage
Operating solely from a foreign country with no NY registration
Having no business address on file ever

explanation: Brokers must maintain a proper place of business and meet registration/office rules.

distractorExplanations.a: A P.O. box alone is typically insufficient when an office is required.
distractorExplanations.c: NY practice requires proper registration/presence rules to be met.
distractorExplanations.d: A business address on file is required.

prompt: Misrepresentation of a material property fact by a licensee can result in:

options.text: Automatic immunity if the buyer liked the kitchen
A required bonus commission from DOS
Civil liability and DOS disciplinary action
Exemption from all consumer protection laws

explanation: Material misrepresentation can trigger discipline and civil claims; there is no immunity for misleading buyers/sellers.

distractorExplanations.a: Buyer preference does not erase misrepresentation.
distractorExplanations.b: Violations do not earn bonus commissions.
distractorExplanations.d: Consumer protection still applies.

prompt: In a single agency relationship representing the seller, the licensee's fiduciary duties are owed primarily to:

options.text: The seller-client
The buyer's lender only
The title company exclusively
Any passerby at the open house

explanation: In seller agency, fiduciary duties (loyalty, confidentiality, etc.) run to the seller-client.

distractorExplanations.b: Lenders are not the agency client in seller agency.
distractorExplanations.c: Title companies are closing parties, not the agency principal.
distractorExplanations.d: Open-house visitors are not automatic clients.

prompt: A listing agreement is best described as:

options.text: A deed transferring title immediately
An employment contract between the seller and the broker authorizing marketing/sale services
A mortgage note
An appraisal report

explanation: Listings hire the brokerage to market the property; they do not convey title or create a loan.

distractorExplanations.a: Deeds convey title.
distractorExplanations.c: Notes evidence debt.
distractorExplanations.d: Appraisals estimate value.

prompt: Which element is essential to form a valid real estate sales contract?

options.text: A handshake with no terms ever
Only a verbal price with no parties identified
Offer, acceptance, consideration, competent parties, and lawful purpose (among other formalities)
A social media like on the listing photo

explanation: Contracts require the classic elements plus applicable writing requirements under the Statute of Frauds.

distractorExplanations.a: Vague handshakes lack enforceable terms.
distractorExplanations.b: Parties and essential terms must be identifiable.
distractorExplanations.d: Likes are not acceptances of contract terms.

prompt: Dual agency in New York generally requires:

options.text: Keeping both parties unaware forever
Automatic dual agency with no disclosure
Dual agency only for cash deals under $10,000
Informed written consent of the parties as required by law

explanation: Advanced informed consent is required before dual agency can lawfully proceed.

distractorExplanations.a: Concealment violates agency law.
distractorExplanations.b: Disclosure/consent is mandatory, not automatic secrecy.
distractorExplanations.c: Price and cash terms do not waive consent rules.

prompt: An exclusive right-to-sell listing typically means:

options.text: The listing broker earns a commission if the property sells during the term, regardless of who finds the buyer (per contract)
No commission is ever owed
Only the seller's cousin can show the home
The broker is paid only if the seller self-negotiates illegally

explanation: Exclusive right-to-sell is the most protective listing form for the broker's commission entitlement.

distractorExplanations.b: Commission is generally due on a qualifying sale.
distractorExplanations.c: Showing rights are governed by the listing, not cousins alone.
distractorExplanations.d: Self-sale during the term usually still owes commission under exclusive right-to-sell.

prompt: The Statute of Frauds generally requires real estate sale contracts to be:

options.text: Only oral forever
In writing and signed by the party to be charged (with limited exceptions)
Written in invisible ink
Signed only by a notary with no parties

explanation: Conveyances and sale contracts for real property generally must be written to be enforceable.

distractorExplanations.a: Oral contracts for land sales are generally unenforceable.
distractorExplanations.c: Visibility/legibility matters for a writing.
distractorExplanations.d: Parties must sign; notary alone is not a substitute party.

prompt: Fair housing law generally prohibits discrimination based on protected classes such as:

options.text: Only credit score and nothing else
Only whether the buyer likes granite counters
Race, color, religion, sex, national origin, familial status, and disability (plus NY/local expansions)
Only the color of the front door

explanation: Federal Fair Housing Act and NY Human Rights Law protect specified classes; NY/local law often adds more.

distractorExplanations.a: Credit may be a legitimate screening factor but is not the fair housing framework.
distractorExplanations.b: Design preferences are not protected-class categories.
distractorExplanations.d: Door color is not a protected class.

prompt: Steering in fair housing means:

options.text: Using GPS to find the listing address
Explaining school district boundaries factually without bias
Providing a MLS printout on request
Guiding prospects toward or away from neighborhoods based on protected-class characteristics

explanation: Steering is unlawful channeling of clients based on protected characteristics.

distractorExplanations.a: Navigation is not fair housing steering.
distractorExplanations.b: Neutral factual information differs from discriminatory guidance.
distractorExplanations.c: MLS data sharing alone is not steering.

prompt: A binder or offer to purchase in New York practice is often:

options.text: A preliminary agreement that may lead to a full contract prepared by attorneys
The final recorded deed
A permanent survey map
A certificate of occupancy

explanation: NY commonly uses binders/offers followed by attorney-prepared contracts; the binder is not the deed.

distractorExplanations.b: Deeds transfer title at closing/recording stages.
distractorExplanations.c: Surveys show boundaries.
distractorExplanations.d: COs relate to occupancy/code compliance.

prompt: Which statement about earnest money is most accurate?

options.text: It always belongs to the broker as an immediate commission
It shows good faith and is typically held in escrow pending contract performance or authorized release
It is illegal in every New York transaction
It replaces the need for a purchase price

explanation: Earnest money is security for performance, not an automatic broker fee or substitute for the purchase price.

distractorExplanations.a: Commission is earned per the listing/agreement, not by seizing escrow.
distractorExplanations.c: Earnest money is common and lawful when handled properly.
distractorExplanations.d: Purchase price remains the consideration for the sale.

prompt: Blockbusting refers to:

options.text: Building a fence on the lot line
Using a lockbox on a listing
Inducing panic selling by suggesting protected-class entry will lower property values
Ordering a title search

explanation: Blockbusting is a prohibited fair housing practice that exploits prejudice to generate listings.

distractorExplanations.a: Fences are property improvements/encroachments issues.
distractorExplanations.b: Lockboxes are showing tools.
distractorExplanations.d: Title searches are closing due diligence.

prompt: A customer (as opposed to a client) is generally owed:

options.text: Absolute obedience to every demand
Confidential advocacy against the client
No honesty whatsoever
Honesty, fairness, and disclosure of known material facts—but not full fiduciary loyalty

explanation: Customers get fair dealing; clients get fiduciary duties. Licensees must still be honest with customers.

distractorExplanations.a: Obedience is a client fiduciary concept within lawful bounds.
distractorExplanations.b: Advocating against your client breaches loyalty.
distractorExplanations.c: Honesty is required with customers.

prompt: An option contract gives the optionee:

options.text: The right, but not the obligation, to purchase on stated terms within a period
An immediate deed without payment
A mandatory duty to buy with no choice
An automatic mortgage approval

explanation: Options create a unilateral right to buy; exercise is optional for the optionee.

distractorExplanations.b: Deeds require conveyance formalities and consideration/performance.
distractorExplanations.c: Options are rights, not obligations, until exercised.
distractorExplanations.d: Lenders approve mortgages separately.

prompt: Which practice violates fair housing advertising rules?

options.text: Stating the number of bedrooms factually
Using phrases that express preference for or against a protected class (e.g., 'no children,' 'Christians only')
Listing the property's square footage
Describing a renovated kitchen accurately

explanation: Ads may describe the property, not discriminatory preferences about who may buy/rent.

distractorExplanations.a: Bedroom count is a property fact.
distractorExplanations.c: Size is a property fact.
distractorExplanations.d: Physical features may be described neutrally.

prompt: If a buyer asks a seller's agent about the seller's motivation, the agent should generally:

options.text: Reveal the lowest price the seller will take without permission
Invent a false story to pressure the buyer
Avoid disclosing confidential client information unless authorized
Hand over the seller's tax returns unsolicited

explanation: Confidentiality protects client motivations and negotiation strategy unless disclosure is authorized or legally required.

distractorExplanations.a: Bottom-line price is confidential negotiation info.
distractorExplanations.b: False statements are misrepresentation.
distractorExplanations.d: Tax returns are private and not casually disclosed.

prompt: Loan-to-value (LTV) ratio is best described as:

options.text: The loan amount divided by the property's value (or purchase price, as used by the lender)
Property taxes divided by HOA dues
Commission rate times days on market
Square footage divided by lot size

explanation: LTV measures leverage: loan ÷ value. Higher LTV generally means more lender risk.

distractorExplanations.b: Taxes/HOA are ownership costs, not LTV.
distractorExplanations.c: Commission and DOM are marketing metrics.
distractorExplanations.d: Size ratios are not financing LTV.

prompt: A conventional conforming mortgage is typically:

options.text: Always an FHA loan
A non-government-insured loan that meets GSE (Fannie/Freddie) guidelines
Always a VA loan
A reverse mortgage only

explanation: Conventional loans are not FHA/VA insured/guaranteed; conforming means they meet agency size/guidelines.

distractorExplanations.a: FHA is government-insured.
distractorExplanations.c: VA is government-guaranteed for eligible veterans.
distractorExplanations.d: Reverse mortgages are a different product.

prompt: PITI commonly stands for:

options.text: Price, Inventory, Title, Inspection
Points, Index, Term, Inflation
Principal, Interest, Taxes, and Insurance
Permit, Impact, Transfer, Income

explanation: PITI is the classic housing payment bundle used in underwriting affordability.

distractorExplanations.a: Those are transaction steps, not PITI.
distractorExplanations.b: Those are loan pricing concepts, not PITI.
distractorExplanations.d: Those are unrelated closing items.

prompt: Which appraisal approach uses recent sales of similar properties?

options.text: Cost approach only
Income capitalization only
Assemblage approach
Sales comparison (market) approach

explanation: The sales comparison approach adjusts comps to estimate market value—most common for residences.

distractorExplanations.a: Cost approach uses replacement cost minus depreciation plus land.
distractorExplanations.b: Income approach capitalizes NOI for income properties.
distractorExplanations.c: Assemblage is combining parcels, not an appraisal approach name here.

prompt: Points paid to a lender at closing typically:

options.text: Are prepaid interest that can reduce the note rate (discount points) or cover fees (origination)
Increase the property's lot size
Eliminate the need for a deed
Automatically waive property taxes forever

explanation: One point = 1% of the loan; discount points buy down rate; origination points are lender fees.

distractorExplanations.b: Points do not change physical land.
distractorExplanations.c: Deeds are still required to convey title.
distractorExplanations.d: Taxes are not waived by points.

prompt: Private mortgage insurance (PMI) is commonly required when:

options.text: The buyer pays 50% down in cash
A conventional loan has a high LTV (often above 80%)
The property is free and clear with no loan
The loan is a 100% equity gift with no mortgage

explanation: PMI protects the lender on high-LTV conventional loans until equity reaches required levels.

distractorExplanations.a: Large down payments usually avoid PMI.
distractorExplanations.c: No loan means no PMI.
distractorExplanations.d: No mortgage means no PMI.

prompt: Amortization means:

options.text: Interest-only forever with no principal reduction
Ignoring the loan balance
Periodic payments that gradually pay down principal over the loan term
Paying only property taxes each month

explanation: Fully amortizing loans retire principal through scheduled P&I payments.

distractorExplanations.a: Interest-only periods delay principal paydown.
distractorExplanations.b: Balances must be tracked and paid.
distractorExplanations.d: Taxes are separate from loan amortization.

prompt: A comparative market analysis (CMA) prepared by a licensee is:

options.text: A USPAP appraisal by definition
A government tax assessment
A title insurance policy
A marketing/pricing opinion using comps—not a formal appraisal

explanation: CMAs help price listings; only licensed/certified appraisers perform appraisals under appraisal standards.

distractorExplanations.a: Appraisals follow appraisal licensing/USPAP frameworks.
distractorExplanations.b: Assessments are for taxation.
distractorExplanations.c: Title policies insure title risk.

prompt: At a typical New York residential closing, the deed is:

options.text: Delivered by the seller (grantor) to the buyer (grantee) to transfer title
Issued by the buyer's employer
Created by the listing photographer
Optional if the parties shake hands

explanation: Title transfers by delivery of a properly executed deed meeting statutory formalities.

distractorExplanations.b: Employers do not convey realty.
distractorExplanations.c: Photos do not transfer title.
distractorExplanations.d: Conveyances require a deed writing.

prompt: Prorations at closing are used to:

options.text: Increase the broker's license fee
Allocate items like taxes and rents fairly between buyer and seller as of the closing date
Cancel the mortgage automatically
Survey the property boundaries

explanation: Prorations split ongoing charges/credits so each party pays their ownership period share.

distractorExplanations.a: License fees are separate DOS matters.
distractorExplanations.c: Mortgage payoff/assumption is separate from proration math.
distractorExplanations.d: Surveys measure land, not money splits.

prompt: An FHA loan is characterized by:

options.text: Being available only to corporations trading on the NYSE
Never requiring any down payment documentation
Mortgage insurance through FHA and more flexible qualifying guidelines for eligible borrowers
Prohibiting owner-occupancy in all cases

explanation: FHA insures lenders; borrowers pay MIP and typically must owner-occupy primary residences under program rules.

distractorExplanations.a: FHA is for eligible individual borrowers, not NYSE listing status.
distractorExplanations.b: Down payment and docs are still required.
distractorExplanations.d: FHA commonly requires owner-occupancy for purchase programs.

prompt: Functional obsolescence is best illustrated by:

options.text: A brand-new roof in excellent condition
A busy highway built next door (external)
Normal wear that is easily cured by paint
An outdated floor plan or inadequate closets that hurt marketability

explanation: Functional obsolescence is loss from outdated/poor design within the property.

distractorExplanations.a: New roofs are physical improvements.
distractorExplanations.b: Highway impact is external/economic obsolescence.
distractorExplanations.c: Deferred maintenance is physical, often curable.

prompt: A balloon payment is:

options.text: A large final payment that pays off remaining principal when the loan does not fully amortize
A monthly tax escrow only
A gift of helium at closing
An HOA initiation fee always

explanation: Partially amortizing loans leave a balance due at maturity—the balloon.

distractorExplanations.b: Tax escrow is separate.
distractorExplanations.c: Party balloons are not loan terms.
distractorExplanations.d: HOA fees are association charges.

prompt: Title insurance primarily protects against:

options.text: Future roof leaks from storms
Covered defects in title that were not discovered in the title search
Furniture damage by movers
Changes in interest rates after closing

explanation: Owner's/lender's title policies insure against covered title risks (liens, forgeries, etc.), not physical property damage.

distractorExplanations.a: Casualty/homeowners insurance covers physical damage.
distractorExplanations.c: Moving damage is not a title risk.
distractorExplanations.d: Rate changes are loan-market risk.

prompt: Which closing document details the final cash due and credits for buyer and seller?

options.text: The listing agreement alone
The broker's pocket card
The Closing Disclosure (TRID) / settlement statement used for the transaction
The open house sign-in sheet

explanation: TRID Closing Disclosures (and settlement statements) itemize funds to close and prorations.

distractorExplanations.a: Listings set brokerage employment, not final settlement figures.
distractorExplanations.b: Pocket cards identify licensees.
distractorExplanations.d: Sign-in sheets track visitors.

prompt: A fixture is generally:

options.text: Personal property that has become real property by annexation/intent/adaptation
Always a free-standing lamp
Cash in a wallet
A car parked in the driveway

explanation: Fixtures (e.g., built-in appliances, chandelier wired in) transfer with the realty unless excluded.

distractorExplanations.b: Free-standing lamps are usually personalty.
distractorExplanations.c: Cash is personal property.
distractorExplanations.d: Vehicles are personal property.

prompt: Fee simple absolute ownership is:

options.text: A lease for one week
The most complete bundle of rights lasting potentially forever
A life estate that ends at death automatically as fee simple
An easement only

explanation: Fee simple is the fullest freehold estate; life estates and leases are more limited.

distractorExplanations.a: Short leases are nonfreehold estates.
distractorExplanations.c: Life estates end/measure by life; they are not fee simple absolute.
distractorExplanations.d: Easements are nonpossessory interests.

prompt: An easement appurtenant typically:

options.text: Is only a personal license that never transfers
Is ownership of the entire servient fee
Benefits a dominant tenement and runs with the land
Is a mortgage lien

explanation: Appurtenant easements attach to land (dominant/servient); they generally transfer with the dominant estate.

distractorExplanations.a: Licenses are typically revocable and personal.
distractorExplanations.b: Easement holders do not own the fee.
distractorExplanations.d: Mortgages are security liens.

prompt: Zoning ordinances primarily regulate:

options.text: Federal income tax rates
Who may hold a salesperson license
Interest rates on mortgages
Land use, density, and building bulk/height within districts

explanation: Zoning is local police-power land-use control; licensing and lending are separate regimes.

distractorExplanations.a: Tax rates are tax authority matters.
distractorExplanations.b: Licensing is DOS.
distractorExplanations.c: Interest rates are lender/market/regulatory finance topics.

prompt: A variance is:

options.text: Authorization to deviate from a zoning requirement due to unnecessary hardship (standards vary)
A type of deed warranty
Automatic rezoning of an entire city
A title insurance endorsement for floods only

explanation: Variances grant relief from strict zoning application in specific hardship cases.

distractorExplanations.b: Deed warranties are conveyance covenants.
distractorExplanations.c: Rezoning changes the map/ordinance more broadly.
distractorExplanations.d: Flood coverage is insurance, not zoning variance.

prompt: Metes and bounds legal descriptions use:

options.text: Only the postal ZIP code
Distances and directions from a point of beginning around a parcel
Only the MLS number
Only the broker's license number

explanation: Metes and bounds are survey-based boundary descriptions common for irregular parcels.

distractorExplanations.a: ZIP codes are delivery areas, not legal descriptions.
distractorExplanations.c: MLS numbers are listing IDs.
distractorExplanations.d: License numbers identify agents, not land.

prompt: Joint tenancy is characterized by:

options.text: No survivorship—interest always goes by will only
Ownership only by corporations forever
Right of survivorship among joint tenants (with unities typically required)
Automatic conversion to a leasehold

explanation: Joint tenancy's hallmark is survivorship; tenancy in common lacks survivorship.

distractorExplanations.a: Survivorship is the defining feature vs TIC.
distractorExplanations.b: Natural persons commonly hold joint tenancy.
distractorExplanations.d: It remains a freehold co-ownership form.

prompt: A lien is:

options.text: A kitchen remodel style
A type of roofing material
A surveyor's measuring tape
A financial claim against property as security for a debt or obligation

explanation: Mortgages, tax liens, and judgments are examples of liens that can encumber title.

distractorExplanations.a: Remodel styles are not liens.
distractorExplanations.b: Roofing is physical construction.
distractorExplanations.c: Tools are personal property.

prompt: Constructive notice of a recorded deed is achieved by:

options.text: Proper recording in the public land records
Whispering the sale to one neighbor
Posting on a private group chat only
Keeping the deed in a drawer forever

explanation: Recording gives constructive notice to the world; unrecorded deeds risk later purchasers without notice.

distractorExplanations.b: Informal word-of-mouth is actual notice at best to hearers.
distractorExplanations.c: Private chats are not public records.
distractorExplanations.d: Unrecorded instruments may not protect against subsequent bona fide purchasers.

prompt: Encroachment is best described as:

options.text: Paying property taxes early
A structure or improvement that illegally extends onto another's land
Recording a mortgage
Obtaining a building permit

explanation: Encroachments (fences, eaves, driveways) create boundary/title issues often found by survey.

distractorExplanations.a: Early tax payment is good practice, not encroachment.
distractorExplanations.c: Recording creates lien notice.
distractorExplanations.d: Permits authorize construction, not trespass.

prompt: In New York condominium ownership, the unit owner typically owns:

options.text: The entire building exclusively including all other units
Only a proprietary lease with no deeded unit (co-op style)
The unit in fee plus an undivided interest in common elements
Nothing but a parking sticker

explanation: Condos convey deeded units plus common-interest shares; co-ops convey shares/leases instead.

distractorExplanations.a: Other units belong to other owners.
distractorExplanations.b: Proprietary leases characterize co-ops, not condos.
distractorExplanations.d: Ownership is far more than parking rights.

prompt: A cooperative (co-op) purchaser in New York typically receives:

options.text: A fee simple deed to a condo unit automatically
Federal land patent title
An easement in gross only
Shares in the cooperative corporation and a proprietary lease

explanation: Co-op buyers buy stock + proprietary lease; they do not get a traditional condo unit deed.

distractorExplanations.a: Condo deeds are a different ownership form.
distractorExplanations.b: Land patents are historical government grants.
distractorExplanations.c: Easements are limited use rights, not co-op ownership.

prompt: Eminent domain is:

options.text: The government's power to take private property for public use with just compensation
A private HOA fine schedule
A broker's right to keep escrow forever
A buyer's right to cancel without contract terms

explanation: Takings require public use/purpose and just compensation under constitutional standards.

distractorExplanations.b: HOA fines are private covenants/rules.
distractorExplanations.c: Escrow misuse is conversion, not eminent domain.
distractorExplanations.d: Cancellation rights come from contracts/statutes, not eminent domain.

prompt: A certificate of occupancy (CO) generally indicates:

options.text: That the mortgage is paid in full
That a building/unit meets applicable codes for occupancy as issued by the authority
That the salesperson passed the licensing exam
That title insurance was declined

explanation: COs are municipal/code compliance documents allowing lawful occupancy.

distractorExplanations.a: Satisfaction of mortgage is a different instrument.
distractorExplanations.c: Licensing is DOS exam/licensing.
distractorExplanations.d: Title insurance decisions are separate.

prompt: Which environmental issue is commonly associated with older homes and disclosure concerns?

options.text: Only brand-new stainless appliances
Fiber-optic internet speed
Lead-based paint (pre-1978 housing) and related federal disclosure rules
The color of blooming flowers

explanation: Federal lead disclosure applies to most pre-1978 residential target housing; other hazards (asbestos, mold) also matter.

distractorExplanations.a: New appliances are not the classic lead issue.
distractorExplanations.b: Internet is not an environmental hazard disclosure category here.
distractorExplanations.d: Landscaping color is not lead paint.
